text: The surgeon demonstrates image inversion during an ETEP procedure to facilitate defect closure. The suprapubic defect is visible, with the right hand initially positioned perpendicular to the defect, making suturing difficult. The left hand has more maneuverability, coming in parallel to the defect. Instruments are swapped, and the camera is rotated and flipped using a touchpad, allowing the former left hand to become the right hand for suturing. This adjustment provides better access and angle for suturing, enabling precise bites of the fascia and hernia sac.

The surgeon is docked on the patient's right side, addressing a lower abdominal defect. The right linea alba is close to the right hand, making suturing difficult due to the perpendicular approach. The surgeon swaps instruments, manually rotates the camera upside down, and uses the touchpad to invert the image, reassigning the master controllers to control the opposite instruments for improved suturing access.

The surgeon uses the XI robot with an image inversion technique to suture the edges of the left and right linea alba. The needle is driven perpendicular to the tissue, taking solid bites of the midline hernia sac and attenuated fascial tissue. The anterior rectus sheath is retracted laterally in front of the rectus muscle, allowing for easier manipulation and suturing.

The surgeon manually rotates the camera to return to a right-side-up view and uses the touchpad to reassign instruments, switching controls for the right and left hands. The midline closure is complete, and the surgeon measures the floor of the surgical site to prepare for mesh placement.
